task: I’ve got a couple of confusing papers from work and I want to make sure I’m not missing anything important. Can you help me figure out what parts of a receipt, ID, or bank form I should check first and what I can safely ignore?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — if you’re trying to quickly spot anything important on a receipt, ID, or bank form, here’s a practical order to check.

## Check first
### 1) Your personal details
- Full name
- Address
- Date of birth
- Account number or customer ID
- Any ID number, if relevant

### 2) Dates
- Issue date
- Expiration date
- Transaction date
- Due date or deadline

### 3) Amounts and totals
- Total charged or paid
- Fees
- Interest
- Balance
- Minimum payment
- Refund amount

### 4) What the document is actually saying
- Transaction description
- Account changes
- Authorization or consent language
- Notice of hold, rejection, or pending action

### 5) Anything that affects your rights or obligations
- Terms and conditions
- Fine print about fees, penalties, or automatic renewal
- Dispute instructions
- Cancellation instructions
- Signature lines
- Approval/denial status

## Usually safe to skim or ignore at first
- Decorative logos and branding
- Repeated headers/footers
- Internal reference codes
- Long legal boilerplate, unless it mentions fees, deadlines, consent, or disputes
- Marketing offers
- Routing or processing notes that don’t affect you directly
- Page numbers and formatting text

## A good quick method
1. Scan for your name and key numbers.
2. Check dates and totals.
3. Look for any “action required,” “due,” “expires,” “fee,” “penalty,” or “dispute” language.
4. Read the signature/authorization section if there is one.

If you want, you can paste the text from the papers here, and I can help you identify the important parts while ignoring the clutter.

user_simulator.text: Yeah, that’s the right stuff. Name, dates, totals, fees, any weird authorization language. The rest is mostly noise unless it says fee, deadline, dispute, or denial. Plain enough.
